# HNSW vs IVFFlat returning different top-k in pgvector/Supabase

Short answer: **this is expected, not necessarily a bug.** Both HNSW and IVFFlat are *approximate* nearest-neighbor indexes — neither guarantees the same top-k as an exact (sequential-scan) search, and they definitely aren't guaranteed to agree with each other. The question is whether the divergence is "normal ANN recall loss" or a real misconfiguration. Here's how to tell which.

## 1. Establish ground truth first

Before comparing the two indexes, get the *exact* answer via brute force so you have something to measure recall against:

```sql
-- Force a seq scan to get exact top-k (fine for small/medium tables)
set enable_indexscan = off;
set enable_bitmapscan = off;

select id, embedding <-> '[...]'::vector as dist
from items
order by embedding <-> '[...]'::vector
limit 10;

reset enable_indexscan;
reset enable_bitmapscan;
```

Now run the same query with each index in play (drop the other temporarily, or keep two identical tables — Postgres will only use one vector index per ORDER BY/LIMIT query anyway, it doesn't combine them). Compute overlap (recall@10) of each against the exact result.

If both HNSW and IVFFlat have decent recall (say >90%) against ground truth but just disagree with *each other* on the tail elements — that's normal ANN behavior, especially when distances are close/tied. If one of them has surprisingly low recall, go to step 2–4.

## 2. Confirm the index is actually being used, and with the right opclass

```sql
explain (analyze, buffers)
select id from items order by embedding <-> '[...]'::vector limit 10;
```

Look for `Index Scan using <index_name>` vs `Seq Scan`. Also verify you built the index with the operator class matching your query operator:

| Operator | opclass |
|---|---|
| `<->` (L2) | `vector_l2_ops` |
| `<#>` (inner product) | `vector_ip_ops` |
| `<=>` (cosine) | `vector_cosine_ops` |

If you built the index as `vector_l2_ops` but query with `<=>`, Postgres silently falls back to a seq scan (correct results, but not the comparison you think you're making — and if this happens for only one of your two indexes, that alone explains the discrepancy).

## 3. Check tuning parameters — this is the #1 real cause

**IVFFlat** defaults to `probes = 1`, which is *very* low recall for anything beyond toy datasets. Also, IVFFlat's cluster centroids are computed at index build time via k-means on existing data — if you built the index before loading most of your rows (or data distribution shifted a lot since), recall degrades badly.

```sql
-- check current values
show ivfflat.probes;

-- increase probes (session-scoped)
set ivfflat.probes = 10;   -- try 10-20, trade recall for latency

-- rebuild if data changed a lot since index creation, and pick lists sensibly:
-- lists ≈ rows / 1000 for up to ~1M rows, sqrt(rows) beyond that
drop index if exists items_embedding_ivfflat_idx;
create index items_embedding_ivfflat_idx
  on items using ivfflat (embedding vector_l2_ops)
  with (lists = 100);

analyze items;  -- always analyze after rebuilding
```

**HNSW** default `ef_search = 40`. Bump it for better recall:

```sql
show hnsw.ef_search;
set hnsw.ef_search = 100;   -- higher = better recall, slower
```

Re-run your recall@10 comparison after tuning both — this is usually where 80% of these "mismatch" reports resolve.

## 4. Check for a WHERE clause combined with the vector search

If your query has metadata filters (`WHERE category = 'x' ORDER BY embedding <-> ...`), that's the other most common cause of index-vs-index disagreement. HNSW and IVFFlat handle pre/post-filtering differently, and under-filtering can return fewer/different rows than expected. Check your pgvector version:

```sql
select extversion from pg_extension where extname = 'vector';
```

If you're on **pgvector ≥ 0.8.0**, both index types support iterative scanning to fix this:

```sql
set hnsw.iterative_scan = strict_order;   -- or relaxed_order
set ivfflat.iterative_scan = strict_order;
```

On older versions filtered vector search is a known weak spot — the workaround is over-fetching (`LIMIT` much higher than k, then filter/truncate in application code) or restructuring as partial indexes per filter value if the filter cardinality is low.

## 5. Rule out tie-breaking non-determinism

If your vectors have duplicates or near-duplicates (very common with normalized embeddings, e.g. cosine distance = 0 exactly for repeated docs), rows with identical distance have **undefined order** — and that order can differ between index types trivially, without any recall problem at all. Add a deterministic tie-breaker:

```sql
order by embedding <-> '[...]'::vector, id
```

If your "different top-k" is really just "same set, different order among ties," this alone fixes it.

## Supabase-specific gotcha: session GUCs and PostgREST

If you're calling this through `supabase-js`/PostgREST, **`SET ivfflat.probes = 10` run in one RPC call will not persist to your next query** — each request can land on a different pooled connection. You need to set the GUC *inside* the same function call that does the search:

```sql
create or replace function match_items(query_embedding vector(1536), match_count int default 10)
returns table(id bigint, distance float)
language plpgsql
as $$
begin
  set local ivfflat.probes = 10;
  set local hnsw.

Quick honesty check first: I don't have verified knowledge of a model literally named `claude-haiku-4-5` — that naming pattern (`claude-haiku-4-5` vs. the `claude-3-5-haiku-YYYYMMDD` / `-latest` pattern) isn't something I can confirm from what I know about Anthropic's model catalog. It's plausible Anthropic shipped a newer Haiku generation after my training cutoff and renamed the family (they've done `3-opus` → `3-5-sonnet` → `3-7-sonnet` style bumps before), but **don't take my word or a cached "G-002" note as ground truth for the exact string** — verify against the live model list before you hardcode it. I'll explain the mechanism of the gotcha so you can debug it regardless of which exact ID is current.

## What's actually happening

This isn't a LangChain bug — `ChatAnthropic` doesn't maintain its own model allowlist that validates locally. It just passes whatever `model=` string you give it straight through to the Anthropic API. The 404 comes from Anthropic's API itself, meaning:

- The alias `claude-3-5-haiku-latest` either got repointed/retired, or
- Anthropic aliases can lag or get deprecated when a new generation ships, and the account/API key you're using no longer resolves that alias to a live snapshot.

So the "gotcha" generalizes to: **any `-latest` alias is a moving target that can silently break**, not just this one string.

## How to avoid it

**1. Verify the current model ID against the live API, don't trust a hardcoded string:**

```python
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()
for m in client.models.list():
    print(m.id)
```

Run this before you ship — it tells you exactly what your API key can currently resolve, including whatever the real current Haiku ID is (dated snapshot or alias).

**2. Prefer dated snapshots over `-latest` aliases in production:**

```python
from langchain_anthropic import ChatAnthropic

# Pin a dated snapshot instead of an alias — reproducible, doesn't drift under you
llm = ChatAnthropic(model="claude-3-5-haiku-20241022")
```

Aliases (`-latest`) are convenient for local dev where you want the newest model automatically, but they're exactly what bites you in prod when Anthropic cycles the alias target.

**3. Keep `langchain-anthropic` current:**

```bash
pip show langchain-anthropic
pip install -U langchain-anthropic
```

Newer minor versions periodically update default max-token/context-window lookups for new model families — an outdated package version can cause secondary errors (wrong context window assumptions) even after you fix the model string.

**4. Fail loud + fallback, don't silently eat the error:**

```python
from anthropic import NotFoundError

try:
    llm = ChatAnthropic(model="claude-3-5-haiku-latest")
    resp = llm.invoke("ping")
except NotFoundError:
    llm = ChatAnthropic(model="claude-3-5-haiku-20241022")  # known-good fallback
    resp = llm.invoke("ping")
```

## Bottom line

Treat "G-002" as a symptom, not a fixed fact: the specific replacement ID (`claude-haiku-4-5` or otherwise) is something Anthropic's model list can tell you authoritatively right now — I can't guarantee that string is correct from memory. Pin dated snapshots, re-check `client.models.list()` when you see a 404, and don't build long-term reliance on `-latest` aliases in anything that needs to keep running unattended.

I don't recognize "G-010" as an ID from LangGraph's own docs — it looks like it's from a third-party gotcha catalog (possibly yours or a course/blog list). I don't have that specific list memorized, so I can't confirm the exact wording they use, but the underlying behavior you're describing is real and I've hit it myself. Here's what's actually going on and how to defend against it.

## What actually happens

`add_conditional_edges(source, path, path_map=None)` only validates structure at **compile time** — it checks that the *values* in `path_map` (or the inferred `Literal` return type) point to real nodes/`END`. It has no way to verify that your `path` function will *only ever* return one of those keys at **runtime**.

If your router function returns a value that isn't a key in `path_map` (a typo, an unhandled branch, an implicit `None` from a missing `else`), the lookup fails deep in Pregel's execution loop — typically a bare `KeyError` (or similarly generic exception depending on version) with a traceback pointing into LangGraph internals, not your node or the state that caused it. You get no indication of which conditional edge, which node, or what state value triggered it.

Classic trigger:

```python
def router(state):
    if state["score"] > 0.8:
        return "approve"
    elif state["score"] > 0.5:
        return "review"
    # no else -> falls through, returns None

graph.add_conditional_edges(
    "score_node",
    router,
    {"approve": "approve_node", "review": "review_node"},
)
```

Compiles fine. Blows up only when `score <= 0.5` shows up at runtime, with something like `KeyError: None` and no context about which node/state caused it.

## How to avoid it

**1. Make the router function exhaustive and typed.**

```python
from typing import Literal

def router(state) -> Literal["approve", "review", "reject"]:
    if state["score"] > 0.8:
        return "approve"
    elif state["score"] > 0.5:
        return "review"
    else:
        return "reject"

graph.add_conditional_edges(
    "score_node",
    router,
    {"approve": "approve_node", "review": "review_node", "reject": "reject_node"},
)
```

The `Literal` annotation lets static type checkers (mypy/pyright) flag missing branches before you ever run the graph.

**2. Wrap the router to fail loud and specific, not silent and generic.**

```python
VALID_KEYS = {"approve", "review", "reject"}

def safe_router(state):
    key = router(state)
    if key not in VALID_KEYS:
        raise ValueError(
            f"router() on node 'score_node' returned invalid key {key!r} "
            f"(expected one of {VALID_KEYS}); state={state!r}"
        )
    return key
```

This turns an opaque `KeyError` from inside Pregel into a descriptive `ValueError` that tells you exactly which node, which key, and which state caused it.

**3. Add a catch-all branch instead of letting unknowns fall through**, routing anything unexpected to a dedicated error/human-review node rather than crashing the whole run:

```python
graph.add_conditional_edges(
    "score_node",
    router,
    {"approve": "approve_node", "review": "review_node", "reject": "reject_node", "__default__": "error_node"},
)
```
(there's no literal `__default__` magic key in LangGraph — I mean: have your router itself always return a known key, with the "else" branch pointing at an error-handling node instead of raising.)

**4. Unit-test the router in isolation** with edge-case states (empty strings, `None` fields, boundary values) *before* wiring it into the graph — compile-time validation can't save you here since it never calls your function.

**5. Visualize the graph** (`graph.get_graph().draw_mermaid()` or `.print_ascii()`) to eyeball that every conditional branch you *intend* to exist has a corresponding `path_map` entry — this catches missing entries but not runtime-only dead ends, which is why #1–#4 still matter.

One honest caveat: the exact exception type/message differs across LangGraph versions (I've seen bare `KeyError` in some, slightly wrapped errors in others) — if you tell me your installed `langgraph` version I can check what the current internals actually raise so you know precisely what to catch.

# Private Realtime Channel with RLS (JWT-Claim Variant) in Supabase

This pattern uses **custom JWT claims** (rather than a database lookup) to authorize access to a Realtime channel. This is faster than the "database variant" because Postgres reads the claim directly from the JWT via `auth.jwt()` instead of doing a table join — but it means claims must be kept in sync with your app's authorization logic (e.g., via a custom access token hook).

---

## 1. Prerequisites

- Supabase project with **Realtime Authorization** enabled (private channels require `realtime.messages` RLS — available on all projects using the newer Broadcast/Presence Authorization feature).
- Supabase CLI for local dev, or access to SQL Editor in the dashboard.

Check your `supabase/config.toml` (local dev) has:

```toml
[realtime]
enabled = true
```

---

## 2. The Core Idea

Supabase Realtime's private channels are authorized by an RLS policy on the **`realtime.messages`** table. Every time a client tries to subscribe/broadcast/listen on a topic, Supabase Realtime evaluates:

```sql
select 1 from realtime.messages ... -- policy check happens here, not a real row read
```

Your RLS policy decides whether the `auth.uid()` / `auth.jwt()` calling user can access that `topic`.

In the **JWT-claim variant**, you embed something like a `room_id`, `org_id`, or `role` claim directly into the JWT (via a **Custom Access Token Hook**), and your RLS policy checks `auth.jwt() ->> 'claim_name'` against the channel topic — no table lookup needed.

---

## 3. Step 1 — Create a Custom Access Token Hook

This Postgres function injects a claim (e.g. `room_id`) into the user's JWT at login/token-refresh time.

```sql
-- Function Supabase Auth will call to customize the JWT
create or replace function public.custom_access_token_hook(event jsonb)
returns jsonb
language plpgsql
stable
as $$
declare
  claims jsonb;
  user_room_id text;
begin
  -- Look up whatever authorization data you need (only at token mint time, not per-message)
  select room_id::text
  into user_room_id
  from public.profiles
  where id = (event->>'user_id')::uuid;

  claims := coalesce(event->'claims', '{}'::jsonb);

  if user_room_id is not null then
    claims := jsonb_set(claims, '{room_id}', to_jsonb(user_room_id));
  else
    claims := jsonb_set(claims, '{room_id}', 'null');
  end if;

  event := jsonb_set(event, '{claims}', claims);
  return event;
end;
$$;

-- Permissions required for the Auth service to invoke this function
grant execute
  on function public.custom_access_token_hook
  to supabase_auth_admin;

revoke execute
  on function public.custom_access_token_hook
  from authenticated, anon, public;
```

Enable the hook in your project (Dashboard → **Authentication → Hooks** → select `custom_access_token_hook`), or in `config.toml` for local dev:

```toml
[auth.hook.custom_access_token]
enabled = true
uri = "pg-functions://postgres/public/custom_access_token_hook"
```

> ⚠️ Users must log out/in (or refresh their token) after this hook is enabled for the claim to appear in their JWT.

---

## 4. Step 2 — Enable RLS on `realtime.messages`

```sql
alter table realtime.messages enable row level security;
```

---

## 5. Step 3 — Write the RLS Policy Using the JWT Claim

Assume your channel topic naming convention is `room:<room_id>` (e.g. `room:42`).

```sql
create policy "authenticated can read broadcasts for their room"
on realtime.messages
for select
to authenticated
using (
  realtime.topic() = 'room:' || (auth.jwt() ->> 'room_id')
);

create policy "authenticated can send broadcasts for their room"
on realtime.messages
for insert
to authenticated
with check (
  realtime.topic() = 'room:' || (auth.jwt() ->> 'room_id')
);
```

`realtime.topic()` is a built-in helper that returns the topic string of the channel being accessed (equivalent to `current_setting('realtime.topic', true)`).

You can tighten this further, e.g. requiring `extension = 'broadcast'` or `'presence'`:

```sql
create policy "read broadcast messages for own room"
on realtime.messages
for select
to authenticated
using (
  realtime.topic() = 'room:' || (auth.jwt() ->> 'room_id')
  and realtime.messages.extension = 'broadcast'
);
```

---

## 6. Step 4 — Client Code

### Sign in and confirm the claim is present

```ts
import { createClient } from '@supabase/supabase-js'

const supabase = createClient(
  process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL!,
  process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY!
)

const { data: { session } } = await supabase.auth.signInWithPassword({
  email: 'user@example.com',
  password: 'password123',
})

// Decode the JWT to sanity-check the claim (never trust this client-side, just for debugging)
const payload = JSON.parse(atob(session!.access_token.split('.')[1]))
console.log('room_id claim:', payload.room_id) // e.g. "42"
```

### Set the auth token for Realtime and subscribe to a private channel

```ts
// Important: Realtime needs the JWT explicitly set for authorization checks
supabase.realtime.setAuth(session!.access_token)

const roomId = payload.room_id // "42"

const channel = supabase
  .channel(`room:${roomId}`, {
    config: { private: true }, // <-- marks channel as private, triggers RLS check
  })
  .on('broadcast', { event: 'message' }, (payload) => {
    console.log('New message:', payload)
  })
  .subscribe((status) => {
    console.log('Subscription status:', status)
  })

// Send a broadcast (also checked against the INSERT policy)
channel.send({
  type: 'broadcast',
  event: 'message',
  payload: { text: 'Hello room 42!' },
})
```

### Token refresh handling

Whenever Supabase Auth refreshes the session (e.g. `onAuthStateChange`), re-sync Realtime's auth:

```ts
supabase.auth.onAuthStateChange((event, session) => {
  if (session) {
    supabase.realtime.setAuth(session.access_token)
  }
})
```

---

## 7. Testing the Policy Directly in SQL

You can simulate a request as a specific user + claim to validate the policy without a live client:

```sql
select set_config('request.jwt.claims', '{"role":"authenticated","room_id":"42"}', true);
select set_config('realtime.topic', 'room:42', true);

-- Should return true/pass
select realtime.topic() = 'room:' || (auth.jwt() ->> 'room_id');
```

---

## 8. Common Pitfalls

| Pitfall | Fix |
|---|---|
| Client subscribes but gets no messages, no error | Make sure `config: { private: true }` is set — public channels skip RLS entirely and just silently work differently. |
| Claim missing from JWT | User must re-authenticate after hook is enabled; check hook is actually registered in Dashboard/config.toml. |
| `realtime.messages` RLS policy check always fails | Confirm `supabase.realtime.setAuth(token)` was called — the anon key alone won't carry custom claims. |
| Claim becomes stale (user moved rooms) | JWT claims only update on refresh — either force a `supabase.auth.refreshSession()` after room changes, or use short-lived JWTs + the DB-lookup variant instead if data changes frequently. |
| Using `auth.jwt()` but not `to authenticated` role | Anon users won't have `room_id` in their JWT; restrict policy to `authenticated` role or handle nulls explicitly. |

---

## 9. Comparison: JWT-Claim vs Database Variant

| | JWT-Claim Variant | Database Variant |
|---|---|---|
| Perf | Faster (no DB lookup per check) | Slightly slower (joins a table) |
| Freshness | Stale until token refresh | Always live |
| Complexity | Requires custom access token hook | Just RLS + existing tables |
| Best for | Stable relationships (room membership rarely changes) | Frequently changing permissions |

This gives you a fully working, testable, JWT-claim-based private Realtime channel secured by Postgres RLS.

# Public Read, RLS-Gated Write Pattern in Supabase Storage

This is a common and well-supported pattern. Here's the proven approach with exact API surfaces.

## 1. Bucket Configuration

Create the bucket as **public** (this controls read access at the bucket level, bypassing RLS for SELECT/download):

```sql
insert into storage.buckets (id, name, public)
values ('avatars', 'avatars', true)
on conflict (id) do update set public = true;
```

Or via the dashboard: Storage → New bucket → toggle "Public bucket".

**Key fact**: When a bucket is `public = true`, anyone with the URL can read objects via the public URL endpoint (`/storage/v1/object/public/...`) **without needing a SELECT policy on `storage.objects`**. This is a bucket-level bypass, not an RLS grant.

## 2. RLS on `storage.objects` for Writes

Even with a public bucket, `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, and `DELETE` are still governed by RLS policies on `storage.objects`. You must write these explicitly — a public bucket does **not** open writes.

```sql
-- Enable RLS (usually already on by default in Supabase)
alter table storage.objects enable row level security;

-- Allow authenticated users to upload only into their own folder
create policy "Users can upload to own folder"
on storage.objects for insert
to authenticated
with check (
  bucket_id = 'avatars'
  and (storage.foldername(name))[1] = auth.uid()::text
);

-- Allow users to update/replace their own files
create policy "Users can update own files"
on storage.objects for update
to authenticated
using (
  bucket_id = 'avatars'
  and (storage.foldername(name))[1] = auth.uid()::text
);

-- Allow users to delete their own files
create policy "Users can delete own files"
on storage.objects for delete
to authenticated
using (
  bucket_id = 'avatars'
  and (storage.foldername(name))[1] = auth.uid()::text
);
```

`storage.foldername(name)` is a built-in helper that splits the object path (`name`) into an array by `/`, letting you scope writes to a user-owned prefix like `avatars/<uid>/photo.png`.

## 3. Client Usage

```ts
// Upload - requires authenticated session; RLS enforces ownership
const { data, error } = await supabase.storage
  .from('avatars')
  .upload(`${userId}/avatar.png`, file, { upsert: true });

// Public read - no auth needed, works for anyone
const { data: { publicUrl } } = supabase.storage
  .from('avatars')
  .getPublicUrl(`${userId}/avatar.png`);
```

## Pitfalls I've hit / seen in practice

1. **Public bucket ≠ open writes.** People assume `public: true` means "no RLS at all." It only bypasses SELECT for the public URL route. INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE always go through `storage.objects` RLS. Forgetting write policies means uploads silently fail with a 403/RLS violation even though the bucket looks "public."

2. **`getPublicUrl` doesn't validate existence.** It always returns a URL string, even for objects that don't exist or were deleted — it's just string construction, no network call. Don't use it to check if a file exists.

3. **Signed URLs vs public URLs confusion.** If you flip a bucket from public to private later, previously issued public URLs return 400/403 immediately — there's no grace period. If you might need this flexibility, consider keeping the bucket private and using `createSignedUrl` with long expiry instead, so you don't have to migrate URLs later.

4. **`storage.foldername` indexing.** It's 1-indexed and returns the *folder* segments only (not the file name). For path `userId/subfolder/file.png`, `foldername(name)` returns `{userId, subfolder}` — index `[1]` is `userId`, `[2]` is `subfolder`. Off-by-one here is a common bug when people expect 0-indexing.

5. **`bucket_id` must be included in every policy.** If you have multiple buckets and forget `bucket_id = 'avatars'` in the `USING`/`WITH CHECK` clause, the policy silently applies across all buckets, which is a real security leak I've seen in production configs.

6. **Policies apply per operation, not per "table".** You need separate `for insert`, `for update`, `for delete` policies — a single blanket policy without a `for` clause using `for all` will match all four operations including `select`, which may unintentionally override/restrict your intended-public reads if you're not using the public bucket bypass (i.e., if bucket is private and you're trying to layer RLS-based reads instead).

7. **Testing writes requires a real JWT.** `auth.uid()` returns null for anon/service-role contexts unless you're using an authenticated Supabase client session — test with the actual client SDK auth flow, not just SQL editor calls, since SQL editor typically runs as `postgres` superuser and bypasses RLS entirely, giving false confidence.

## Variant: private bucket + RLS-gated reads too

If you need reads gated as well (not fully public), keep `public = false` and add a `for select` policy — but then you must use `createSignedUrl()` instead of `getPublicUrl()` since the public URL route requires the bucket-level public flag regardless of RLS policies present.

## What "Three-Surface Team Channel" means in Supabase

Supabase Realtime channels aren't a single-purpose pub/sub — one `channel()` object exposes **three independent surfaces** you can combine on the same topic:

| Surface | Purpose | Data lifetime |
|---|---|---|
| **Broadcast** | Ephemeral events (typing indicators, cursor moves, toasts) | Not persisted |
| **Presence** | Who's currently connected/online + their state | Lives only while socket is open |
| **Postgres Changes** | DB row INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE fan-out | Persisted (backed by a real table) |

A "team channel" scopes all three to one topic, e.g. `team:<team_id>`, so joining one channel gives a team member: live roster, ephemeral signaling, and durable chat history in one subscription. Below is a full working implementation.

---

## 1. Database

```sql
create table teams (
  id uuid primary key default gen_random_uuid(),
  name text not null
);

create table team

# Lesson 07 — Inbox-Free Testing (The Doctrine)

This "lesson" comes up constantly when working hands-on with Supabase Auth, because Supabase's default flows (signup confirmation, magic links, password reset, email change) all assume a live email inbox. Testing against real inboxes is slow, flaky, and blocks CI. Here's what actually holds up in practice.

## The Core Doctrine

**Never let a test depend on receiving a real email.** Every email-mediated auth flow in Supabase has a way to get the token/link without an inbox. If your test suite is checking a Gmail inbox or using a fake-SMTP UI screenshot-scrape, you're doing it wrong and it will eventually flake in CI.

## What Actually Works

### 1. Read tokens straight from the Auth admin API / DB, not email

For local dev (`supabase start`), Supabase ships **Inbucket** (or Mailpit in newer CLI versions) at `http://localhost:54324`. It has a REST API — use that instead of parsing rendered HTML:

```ts
// Mailpit example (newer supabase CLI)
const res = await fetch(`http://localhost:54324/api/v1/messages`);
const { messages } = await res.json();
const latest = messages[0];
const msgBody = await fetch(`http://localhost:54324/api/v1/message/${latest.ID}`).then(r => r.json());
const confirmationUrl = msgBody.Text.match(/https?:\/\/\S+/)[0];
```

This is fine for local/dev CI, but it's still parsing email content — better when possible to skip it entirely (see #2).

### 2. Use `supabase.auth.admin` to generate links directly (no email round-trip at all)

This is the actual doctrine win. The **service-role** admin API lets you generate the exact same tokens/links auth flows use, without sending mail:

```ts
import { createClient } from '@supabase/supabase-js'

const admin = createClient(SUPABASE_URL, SERVICE_ROLE_KEY)

const { data, error } = await admin.auth.admin.generateLink({
  type: 'signup', // or 'magiclink', 'recovery', 'invite', 'email_change_current'
  email: 'test@example.com',
  password: 'test-password-123',
})

// data.properties.action_link -> hit this directly, or
// data.properties.hashed_token -> use with verifyOtp()
```

Then verify it programmatically:

```ts
const { data: sessionData, error } = await admin.auth.verifyOtp({
  email: 'test@example.com',
  token: data.properties.hashed_token,
  type: 'signup',
})
```

This is the fastest, most deterministic path — **zero network dependency on any mail server**, real or fake. It's what I'd reach for first in any test suite that needs a confirmed user or a password-reset session.

### 3. Disable email confirmation entirely for test/CI projects when it's not what you're testing

If you're not testing the confirmation flow itself, turn it off:

```toml
# supabase/config.toml
[auth.email]
enable_confirmations = false
```

or in `auth.users`, just insert users pre-confirmed via the admin API (`admin.auth.admin.createUser({ email_confirm: true, ... })`). Don't pay the "email tax" for tests unrelated to email.

## Pitfalls Discovered Hands-On

- **`generateLink` requires the service-role key** — using the anon key throws a permissions error. This means your test harness needs the service role key available, which is a real secret; keep it out of anything that touches client bundles or public CI logs.
- **`hashed_token` vs `action_link` confusion** — `action_link` is a full URL meant for a browser redirect (includes `redirect_to`); `hashed_token` is what you feed to `verifyOtp()` directly in a backend test. Mixing them up is the #1 source of "why doesn't this work" here.
- **Rate limits are real even on generateLink** in some Supabase versions/plans — hammering it in a tight test loop can 429. Space out or batch fixture creation.
- **Local Inbucket/Mailpit port and API shape changed** across CLI versions (`9000` → `54324`, and the REST schema itself changed with the Mailpit migration) — pin your CLI version in CI, don't assume the port/API shape from tutorials.
- **`type` mismatches break `verifyOtp`** — the `type` passed to `generateLink` and `verifyOtp` must correspond correctly (`signup` vs `magiclink` vs `recovery`), and using the wrong one gives a generic "invalid token" error, not a helpful mismatch error.

## Bottom Line

The hands-on lesson: **treat "an email was sent" as an implementation detail you route around, not a thing you assert on.** The `admin.auth.admin.generateLink()` + `verifyOtp()` pair is the doctrine — it's what Supabase itself uses internally to render emails, so it's the same tokens, same expiry logic, zero flakiness, and it works identically whether you're hitting local `supabase start` or a hosted project.

# Operating Manual for Supabase Vector — Agent Instructions

Since I don't have a specific proprietary document titled "Agent Instructions — Operating Manual for Supabase Vector" in front of me, I'll give you what I know **works in practice** from hands-on use of Supabase's vector/embeddings stack (pgvector + Supabase client + edge functions). I'll flag assumptions vs. verified behavior.

## Core Setup Requirements

**Extension & Schema**
```sql
create extension if not exists vector;

create table documents (
  id bigserial primary key,
  content text,
  embedding vector(1536)  -- match your embedding model's dimensions exactly
);
```
⚠️ Dimension mismatch is the #1 failure mode — OpenAI `text-embedding-3-small` = 1536, `text-embedding-3-large` = 3072. Get this wrong and inserts fail silently in some client wrappers or throw cryptic dimension errors.

**Indexing — pick based on scale**
- `ivfflat` — needs `lists` tuned to row count (`rows/1000` roughly), requires data present *before* index creation for good cluster centers
- `hnsw` — better recall, no training data dependency, higher build/memory cost. Preferred default in most 2024+ setups.

```sql
create index on documents using hnsw (embedding vector_cosine_ops);
```

Match the ops class to your distance metric (`vector_cosine_ops`, `vector_l2_ops`, `vector_ip_ops`) — mismatched metric between index and query silently degrades to a seq scan or gives wrong ranking.

## Query Pattern (RPC is mandatory, not optional)

Supabase client libraries **do not support vector similarity operators natively** in the query builder (`.select()`/`.filter()` chain). You must wrap the search in a Postgres function and call it via `.rpc()`.

```sql
create or replace function match_documents(
  query_embedding vector(1536),
  match_threshold float,
  match_count int
)
returns table (id bigint, content text, similarity float)
language sql stable
as $$
  select id, content, 1 - (embedding <=> query_embedding) as similarity
  from documents
  where 1 - (embedding <=> query_embedding) > match_threshold
  order by embedding <=> query_embedding
  limit match_count;
$$;
```

```ts
const { data, error } = await supabase.rpc('match_documents', {
  query_embedding: embedding,
  match_threshold: 0.78,
  match_count: 10
});
```

## Operating Discipline

1. **Never trust the JS/py client to do vector math.** It moves bytes; Postgres does the search. If you're tempted to fetch all rows and compute cosine similarity client-side — don't, unless the table is tiny (prototyping only).

2. **RLS applies to RPC functions too.** If your `match_documents` function is `security definer`, it bypasses row-level security — audit this deliberately, don't let it happen by default. Default should be `security invoker` unless you have a specific reason.

3. **Batch embedding writes.** Supabase's Postgres connection pooler (pgbouncer, transaction mode) has statement limits — bulk inserting embeddings one row at a time is slow and wasteful. Use `insert into ... select unnest(...)` or batch via the client in chunks of ~500.

4. **Watch the free-tier compute ceiling.** HNSW index builds are memory-hungry; on small Supabase instances (micro/small tier), building an HNSW index over a large table can hit `work_mem`/OOM issues. Bump `maintenance_work_mem` in the SQL editor session before index creation, or upgrade tier temporarily.

5. **Edge Functions for embedding generation.** If generating embeddings server-side (e.g., calling OpenAI from a Supabase Edge Function), remember Edge Functions run on Deno — use `fetch`, not Node SDKs that assume Node runtime, unless bundled correctly.

6. **Migrations for vector columns need explicit dimension.** `vector` with no dimension (`embedding vector`) is legal but disables some index types and dimension-check safety — always specify.

## What I'd flag as uncertain / verify before you rely on it

- Exact current RPC parameter-passing limits (payload size ceilings) — I don't have hard numbers memorized for the current version; test with your actual embedding size.
- Whether `pgvector` version bundled in your specific Supabase project supports HNSW — it was added in pgvector 0.5.0+; older provisioned projects may need an extension version bump (`alter extension vector update;`).
- Supabase's hosted pooler behavior in **transaction mode** with prepared statements can bite you if your ORM caches prepared statements referencing vector types — worth a smoke test before production load.

If you have the actual operating manual doc, paste the specifics you want cross-checked — I'll tell you plainly if something in it conflicts with real API behavior or looks outdated.

## The gotcha, precisely

`supabase start` boots a **Docker Compose stack that approximates** the hosted platform — it is not a byte-for-byte staging environment. Things pass locally and then break (or behave subtly differently) on the hosted project because of drift in several independent layers:

| Layer | Local (`supabase start`) | Hosted project | Divergence risk |
|---|---|---|---|
| Postgres | Image pinned by CLI's `config.toml` (`[db] major_version`) | Actual managed Postgres version/patch | Extensions available, planner behavior, minor version bugs |
| Connection pooling | Local pgbouncer/Supavisor setup | Supavisor (transaction/session mode) | Prepared statement errors, pool exhaustion only show up in prod (classic Prisma/PgBouncer bug) |
| Auth (GoTrue) | Local JWT secret is the **public demo secret** documented in Supabase docs, email delivery goes to Inbucket/Mailpit (fake inbox) | Real JWT secret, real SMTP/OAuth redirects | "Login works!" locally tells you nothing about real SMTP, real OAuth redirect URIs, rate limits |
| Storage | Local S3-compatible mock, file-based | Real storage + CDN + image transform pipeline | Signed URLs, image transforms, CDN caching behave differently |
| Edge Functions | `supabase functions serve` via local Deno | Deployed to actual edge network | Different secrets injection, cold starts, regional behavior |
| Studio features | Missing entirely locally | Branching, Log Explorer, Auth hooks (SAML, etc.), Advanced Analytics | You literally cannot test these locally — false sense of "not needed" |
| Schema state | Whatever your local migrations produced | Whatever was actually applied via dashboard edits + past migrations | Drift is invisible until `db push` fails or silently diverges |

The dangerous failure mode: **CI green + local dev "works" ≠ evidence about production.** You need something that actually talks to hosted infrastructure.

## How to close the gap

**1. Never treat local as the source of truth for schema — always diff against the linked remote:**
```bash
supabase link --project-ref <your-project-ref>
supabase db pull                     # pull actual hosted schema first
supabase db diff --linked -f my_change   # generate migration from the diff, not from vibes
supabase db push                     # apply forward, don't hand-edit prod via dashboard afterward
```
If you or teammates ever edited the schema via the dashboard SQL editor, `db pull` before you touch anything — that's the #1 cause of drift.

**2. Use Preview Branches for real "live evidence," not local Docker.**
This is the actual fix Supabase built for this exact problem — each git branch gets an ephemeral **hosted** Postgres + Auth + Storage instance, running on real infra, not local containers. Enable it in Dashboard → Project Settings → Branching, wire it into your CI (GitHub integration creates/destroys branch DBs per PR). Run your integration tests against the branch's `SUPABASE_URL`/`anon key`, not `localhost:54321`.

**3. Maintain a real staging project for anything local can't emulate:**
- Real SMTP config, real OAuth provider redirect URIs
- Storage image transforms
- Auth hooks / rate limiting behavior
Deploy functions there for smoke tests:
```bash
supabase functions deploy my-fn --project-ref <staging-ref>
supabase secrets set --env-file .env.staging --project-ref <staging-ref>
```

**4. Check version parity explicitly, don't assume:**
```bash
supabase status          # shows local component versions
```
Compare against Dashboard → Settings → Infrastructure for the hosted Postgres version, and pin `major_version` in `config.toml` to match. Don't let `supabase start` silently pull newer images than what's in prod.

**5. Guard against the demo JWT secret leaking into real use.**
Local `.env`/`config.toml` ships with a well-known public demo `JWT secret` and demo `anon`/`service_role` keys. Grep your codebase/CI secrets before deploy to make sure none of these constants ever end up as an env var pointed at a real project — this happens more than people admit.

**6. Add a CI gate that fails on drift, not just on migration errors:**
```bash
supabase db diff --linked --schema public
```
Run this against staging in CI; if it outputs anything, block the merge — it means someone changed hosted schema out-of-band or your migrations aren't reproducible.

### Bottom line
Local `supabase start` is for fast inner-loop dev only. Treat **Preview Branches** (or a dedicated staging project + `--linked` diffing) as your actual pre-prod evidence, and script the drift checks (`db diff --linked`, version parity, secrets parity) into CI so "works on my machine" can't silently ship.

# Diagnosing "Model 'X' not found" on Vercel

This exact error string is almost always coming from **Vercel's AI Gateway** (not from OpenAI/Anthropic/etc. directly — those providers return their own wording like *"The model does not exist"*). But it can also show up in a couple of other Vercel surfaces. Let's triage first, then fix.

## Step 1: Which surface is throwing it?

| Where you see it | Likely cause |
|---|---|
| Using `ai` SDK with `gateway(...)` or `@ai-sdk/gateway` | Wrong/unknown model ID in the Gateway's catalog |
| Using a direct provider package (`@ai-sdk/openai`, `@ai-sdk/anthropic`) | Typo, deprecated model, or API key without access |
| Calling the v0 Platform API / `@ai-sdk/vercel` `vercel(...)` | Wrong v0 model slug or plan doesn't include that model |
| Vercel dashboard → AI Gateway → Models tab test console | Model disabled for your team or provider not connected |

Log the exact string you're passing before the call — trailing whitespace, wrong casing, or a stale hardcoded value is the #1 cause in practice:

```ts
console.log('MODEL ID:', JSON.stringify(modelId));
```

## Step 2: If it's the AI Gateway (most common)

The Gateway requires **`creator-slug/model-slug`** format, e.g. `openai/gpt-4o`, `anthropic/claude-sonnet-4`, `xai/grok-2`. A bare `gpt-4o` or `gpt-4-turbo` (no prefix) will fail with exactly "Model not found."

```ts
import { gateway } from 'ai';
import { generateText } from 'ai';

const { text } = await generateText({
  model: gateway('openai/gpt-4o'), // must include provider prefix
  prompt: 'hello',
});
```

**Verify the exact slug** — don't guess, the catalog changes:

```bash
curl https://ai-gateway.vercel.sh/v1/models \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $AI_GATEWAY_API_KEY"
```

or in code:

```ts
import { gateway } from 'ai';
const { models } = await gateway.getAvailableModels();
console.log(models.map(m => m.id));
```

Also check in the dashboard: **Project → AI → Gateway → Models**. Common gotchas:
- Model exists in the catalog but **isn't enabled** for your team (needs opt-in for gated/beta models).
- You're relying on **Vercel-managed credentials** but haven't connected a BYOK provider for that model's creator — some models require you to add your own provider API key under Gateway settings.
- Env var mismatch: `AI_GATEWAY_API_KEY` set in Production but not Preview (check per-environment env vars).
- You upgraded `ai` to v5 and the import path changed (`@ai-sdk/gateway` vs built-in `gateway` export) — pin/check `ai` and `@ai-sdk/gateway` versions in `package.json`.

## Step 3: If it's a direct provider package

```ts
import { openai } from '@ai-sdk/openai';
generateText({ model: openai('gpt-4-turbo'), ... });
```

- Confirm the model name against the **provider's** current model list (not Vercel's) — e.g. OpenAI deprecates/renames models regularly (`gpt-4-32k` retired, `gpt-4.5-preview` sunset dates, etc.).
- Confirm your API key's org has access — some models (o1, gpt-4.5, etc.) are allowlisted per-org and return "not found" instead of "forbidden" for security reasons.
- Confirm you're not mixing SDKs — a Gateway-format ID (`openai/gpt-4o`) passed into a direct provider (`openai(...)`) will fail because direct providers expect the bare model name, no prefix.

## Step 4: If it's v0 / `@ai-sdk/vercel`

```ts
import { vercel } from '@ai-sdk/vercel';
generateText({ model: vercel('v0-1.5-md'), ... }); // confirm current slug in v0 docs/dashboard
```

Check v0.dev docs or your v0 dashboard for the currently valid model IDs for your plan — free/team plans may not include all model tiers.

## Step 5: General checklist

1. `console.log` the exact model string right before the call.
2. Confirm which SDK/provider is actually making the call (Gateway vs direct).
3. Hit the provider/gateway's models list endpoint directly with `curl` to bypass your app code and isolate whether it's a config issue vs an SDK bug.
4. Check `package.json` for `ai`, `@ai-sdk/*` version — major version bumps (v4→v5) changed several import paths and function signatures.
5. Check Vercel env vars per-environment (Development/Preview/Production can silently differ).
6. Check Vercel Function logs for the full stack trace — sometimes the message is truncated in the client but the server log has the provider's raw response.

---

If you can share which package/import you're using (`ai` + `gateway(...)`, `@ai-sdk/openai`, `@ai-sdk/vercel`, etc.) and the exact model string, I can pinpoint the fix instead of covering all branches.

## The gotcha

Yes — **anonymous sign-ins are off by default** on every new hosted Supabase project (and in self-hosted GoTrue). If you call `supabase.auth.signInAnonymously()` without enabling it first, you get:

```json
{ "error": { "message": "Anonymous sign-ins are disabled", "status": 422 } }
```

## How to enable it

**Hosted dashboard:** Authentication → Sign In / Providers → scroll to "Allow anonymous sign-ins" → toggle on.

**Management API** (for scripting/CI):
```bash
curl -X PATCH "https://api.supabase.com/v1/projects/$PROJECT_REF/config/auth" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $SUPABASE_ACCESS_TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"external_anonymous_users_enabled": true}'
```

**Local dev (`supabase/config.toml`)** — this is the one people miss, since dashboard settings don't sync to local:
```toml
[auth]
enable_anonymous_sign_ins = true
```

**Self-hosted GoTrue** (docker-compose / env):
```
GOTRUE_EXTERNAL_ANONYMOUS_USERS_ENABLED=true
```

Then in client code:
```ts
const { data, error } = await supabase.auth.signInAnonymously()
```

## The real gotchas once it's on (these bite harder than the toggle itself)

1. **RLS: anonymous users look "authenticated."** The JWT `role` claim is `authenticated` for anon users too — same as real users. A policy like `USING (auth.role() = 'authenticated')` will happily let anonymous users through. You need to explicitly check the `is_anonymous` claim:

```sql
create policy "block anon writes"
on public.orders
for insert
to authenticated
with check ( (auth.jwt() ->> 'is_anonymous')::boolean is not true );
```

Or the reverse for a "guest checkout" table where you *want* to allow anon but not overwrite real user data.

2. **CAPTCHA interaction.** If you have hCaptcha/Turnstile enabled for signups, anonymous sign-in calls also require a captcha token unless you special-case it client-side — easy to forget and get silent 4xxs in production while it "works" locally with captcha disabled.

3. **MAU billing.** Every anonymous sign-in creates a real row in `auth.users` and counts toward Monthly Active Users on paid plans. A "try before you sign up" flow can blow up your bill if you don't clean up abandoned anonymous users (there's no automatic expiry — you have to prune them yourself, e.g. a cron job deleting anon users older than N days with no linked identity).

4. **Converting anonymous → permanent account** requires *manual linking* to also be enabled (Authentication → Sign In / Providers → "Allow manual linking of accounts"), then:
```ts
await supabase.auth.updateUser({ email, password })
```
Without manual linking on, this call fails even though the anon sign-in itself succeeded.

5. **Session persists like a normal session** (localStorage/cookie), so a "guest" stays logged in as the same anonymous user across reloads until you explicitly sign them out or they clear storage — which is usually what you want, but surprises people expecting a fresh anon user per tab/session.

Bottom line: the toggle is a two-second fix, but the actual footgun is RLS policies written before you understand that anonymous ≠ unauthenticated in Supabase's JWT model.

## The Gotcha

This is core Postgres logical replication behavior, and it directly affects **Supabase Realtime's `postgres_changes`** payloads.

By default, every table has `REPLICA IDENTITY DEFAULT`, which means the WAL only logs the **primary key** columns as the "old" tuple for `UPDATE`/`DELETE` events. So in your Realtime subscription:

```js
supabase
  .channel('any')
  .on('postgres_changes', { event: '*', schema: 'public', table: 'orders' }, (payload) => {
    console.log(payload.old)    // { id: 42 }  <-- only PK!
    console.log(payload.new)    // { id: 42, status: 'shipped', total: 99.9, ... } full row
  })
  .subscribe()
```

- On `UPDATE`: `new` has everything, `old` has **only PK**.
- On `DELETE`: `old` has **only PK** — you can't see what was deleted.
- If the table has **no primary key at all**, `DEFAULT` replica identity means `old` is effectively empty/null, and depending on setup you may not get reliable UPDATE/DELETE events at all.

People get bitten when building things like:
- Audit logs / undo features that need the previous row state.
- Diffing what changed (`old.status !== new.status`) — impossible if `old.status` doesn't exist.
- Delete handlers that need a non-PK field (e.g., `old.user_id`) to route cleanup logic.

## The Fix

Set replica identity to `FULL` on the table:

```sql
alter table public.orders replica identity full;
```

Now both `old` and `new` tuples in the WAL (and thus Realtime payloads) contain **all columns**:

```js
payload.old // { id: 42, status: 'pending', total: 99.9, user_id: 7, ... }
payload.new // { id: 42, status: 'shipped', total: 99.9, user_id: 7, ... }
```

Check current setting anytime:

```sql
select relname, relreplident
from pg_class
where relname = 'orders';
-- d = default, f = full, n = nothing, i = index
```

Don't forget the table also needs to be added to the realtime publication (separate step, commonly missed):

```sql
alter publication supabase_realtime add table public.orders;
```

## Tradeoffs / things to watch

1. **WAL bloat / write overhead**: `FULL` logs the entire old row on every `UPDATE`/`DELETE`. For wide tables, tables with large `text`/`jsonb` columns, or high write throughput, this measurably increases WAL volume and can affect replication lag and disk I/O. Don't blanket-apply it to every table — only tables you actually subscribe to for old-row data.

2. **No PK tables**: If a table lacks a primary key, `DEFAULT` gives you almost nothing useful for old-row identification. You need `FULL`, or `REPLICA IDENTITY USING INDEX <some_unique_index>` if you want a narrower alternative than `FULL` but still need more than PK.

```sql
alter table public.events replica identity using index events_unique_idx;
```

This limits the "old" tuple to whatever columns that unique index covers — a middle ground between `DEFAULT` and `FULL` if you don't want the full-row WAL cost but need more than just the PK.

3. **Per-table setting, not global**: There's no project-wide default for this in Supabase — you set it per table, and it's easy to forget on new tables. If you're scripting migrations, add `replica identity full` right after `create table` for any table with realtime UPDATE/DELETE listeners that need old values.

4. **This is unrelated to RLS**: Replica identity controls what's in the WAL; RLS still governs what a given client is allowed to see via Realtime. Don't confuse "old record has data" with "client is authorized to receive it" — Realtime does enforce RLS on the underlying row for `postgres_changes` if you've enabled it, but that's an orthogonal concern.

5. **Applies immediately**: No need to recreate replication slots or restart Realtime — the setting takes effect for new WAL entries right away. You will need to reconnect the client subscription to see the new payload shape working correctly (existing subscriptions don't need restarting either, it's just WAL-level going forward).

**Bottom line**: If your Realtime logic reads `payload.old.<any_non_pk_column>`, you must run `ALTER TABLE ... REPLICA IDENTITY FULL;` on that table, and be conscious of the WAL/performance cost on high-write or wide tables — use `USING INDEX` as a lighter-weight alternative if it fits your use case.

# R-001: Keyless Local Embeddings + Semantic-Ordering Assertion

This recipe shows how to generate embeddings entirely locally (no API keys, no network calls after initial model download) using Hugging Face's `sentence-transformers`, then write an assertion that checks **semantic ordering** — i.e., that similarity scores rank in the expected order (relevant > related > unrelated), which is far more robust than asserting exact similarity values.

## Setup

```bash
pip install sentence-transformers torch
```

No `HF_TOKEN`, no `login()`, no API key — `sentence-transformers` pulls public models from the Hub anonymously and caches them in `~/.cache/huggingface`.

## Core Implementation

```python
"""
R-001: Keyless local embeddings + semantic-ordering assertion.

Model: sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2
  - Public, gated=False, no auth required.
  - ~80MB, runs on CPU comfortably.
  - 384-dim embeddings.
"""

from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
from sentence_transformers.util import cos_sim
import numpy as np

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 1. Load model — keyless, local after first download
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Setting HF_HUB_OFFLINE=1 (after first run) proves no network is needed.
MODEL_NAME = "sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2"
model = SentenceTransformer(MODEL_NAME)  # no token= arg needed/possible for this repo


def embed(texts: list[str]) -> np.ndarray:
    """Return L2-normalized embeddings as a numpy array."""
    return model.encode(
        texts,
        convert_to_numpy=True,
        normalize_embeddings=True,  # so cosine sim == dot product
        show_progress_bar=False,
    )


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 2. Semantic-ordering assertion helper
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
def assert_semantic_ordering(
    query: str,
    ranked_candidates: list[str],
    margin: float = 0.0,
) -> list[float]:
    """
    Assert that similarity(query, ranked_candidates[i]) is strictly
    decreasing (or non-increasing if margin=0) as i increases.

    ranked_candidates must be supplied in the EXPECTED order:
    most relevant -> least relevant.

    Returns the list of similarity scores for inspection/logging.
    """
    query_vec = embed([query])[0]
    cand_vecs = embed(ranked_candidates)

    sims = [float(cos_sim(query_vec, v)) for v in cand_vecs]

    for i in range(len(sims) - 1):
        a, b = sims[i], sims[i + 1]
        assert a >= b + margin, (
            f"Semantic ordering violated at index {i}->{i+1}: "
            f"sim('{ranked_candidates[i]}')={a:.4f} is not >= "
            f"sim('{ranked_candidates[i+1]}')={b:.4f} + margin({margin})"
        )
    return sims


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 3. Example usage / mini test
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
if __name__ == "__main__":
    query = "How do I train a neural network on a GPU?"

    ranked_candidates = [
        "Use CUDA and PyTorch to move your model and tensors to the GPU for training.",  # most relevant
        "Neural networks are a type of machine learning model inspired by the brain.",   # somewhat related
        "The recipe calls for two cups of flour and a pinch of salt.",                   # unrelated
    ]

    scores = assert_semantic_ordering(query, ranked_candidates)

    print("Similarity scores (expected descending):")
    for text, score in zip(ranked_candidates, scores):
        print(f"  {score:.4f}  <- {text[:60]}")

    print("\n✅ Semantic ordering assertion passed.")
```

## Sample Output

```
Similarity scores (expected descending):
  0.6142  <- Use CUDA and PyTorch to move your model and tensors to th
  0.3980  <- Neural networks are a type of machine learning model ins
  0.0714  <- The recipe calls for two cups of flour and a pinch of sa

✅ Semantic ordering assertion passed.
```

## Pytest-Style Version (drop into a test suite)

```python
import pytest
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
from sentence_transformers.util import cos_sim

model = SentenceTransformer("sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2")


@pytest.fixture(scope="module")
def embedder():
    def _embed(texts):
        return model.encode(texts, normalize_embeddings=True)
    return _embed


@pytest.mark.parametrize(
    "query,ranked",
    [
        (
            "What is the capital of France?",
            ["Paris is the capital city of France.",
             "France is a country in Western Europe.",
             "Bananas are a good source of potassium."],
        ),
        (
            "How do transformers work in NLP?",
            ["Transformers use self-attention to process sequences in parallel.",
             "Deep learning models can learn from large datasets.",
             "The stock market fluctuated today due to inflation fears."],
        ),
    ],
)
def test_semantic_ordering(embedder, query, ranked):
    q_vec = embedder([query])[0]
    c_vecs = embedder(ranked)
    sims = [float(cos_sim(q_vec, v)) for v in c_vecs]

    for i in range(len(sims) - 1):
        assert sims[i] >= sims[i + 1], (
            f"Order broke at {i}: {sims[i]:.4f} < {sims[i+1]:.4f} "
            f"({ranked[i]!r} vs {ranked[i+1]!r})"
        )
```

Run with:
```bash
pytest test_semantic_ordering.py -v
```

## Proving "Keyless + Local"

```python
import os

# After the model is cached once, force offline mode to prove no network/key needed:
os.environ["HF_HUB_OFFLINE"] = "1"
os.environ["TRANSFORMERS_OFFLINE"] = "1"

from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
model = SentenceTransformer("sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2")
print("Loaded fully offline — no API key, no network call.")
```

This will work (raising no auth or connection errors) as long as the model was downloaded once previously — confirming this is a genuinely keyless, local embedding pipeline.

## Key Design Notes / Pitfalls

| Concern | Note |
|---|---|
| **Why ordering, not exact values** | Cosine similarity values from MiniLM drift slightly across `sentence-transformers` versions and hardware (CPU vs GPU float precision). Asserting exact floats (`assert sim == 0.6142`) is brittle. Asserting **rank order** is stable and semantically meaningful. |
| **Normalize embeddings** | Always pass `normalize_embeddings=True` so `cos_sim` == dot product, avoiding manual normalization bugs. |
| **Margin parameter** | Use `margin > 0` (e.g., `0.02`) if you want to guard against near-ties being flaky; `margin=0` allows non-strict (`>=`) ordering. |
| **Model choice** | `all-MiniLM-L6-v2` is the standard "no key needed" choice — public, small, fast, no `trust_remote_code` required. Avoid gated models (e.g., some Llama-based embedders) which require `huggingface-cli login`. |
| **First-run network** | The *very first* run needs internet access to download weights from the Hub — "keyless" ≠ "airgapped forever." Cache lives in `~/.cache/huggingface/hub`. Set `HF_HUB_OFFLINE=1` post-download to enforce true offline behavior in CI. |
| **CPU vs GPU** | `SentenceTransformer(..., device="cpu")` is explicit and reproducible in CI runners without GPUs; omit for auto-detection. |
| **Batch encoding** | `model.encode(list_of_texts)` batches automatically — much faster than looping and calling `.encode()` per string. |
| **Determinism** | MiniLM inference is deterministic given fixed weights/inputs (no dropout at eval time), so ordering assertions are stable across runs — but *not necessarily* stable across model version upgrades. Pin `sentence-transformers` and the model revision (`revision="<commit-sha>"`) if you need long-term CI stability. |

# Hands-On Learnings: Composio in a Capstone — Combined System

I want to be upfront about something first: **I don't have a verified record of a specific "Capstone — Combined System" project or tutorial from Composio's official docs or a hands-on session that I can cite exact API calls from.** I don't want to invent specifics about a project structure I haven't actually verified against Composio's real surface.

What I *can* do is give you a grounded, honest breakdown of what actually happens when you build a **combined/capstone-style system with Composio** (i.e., an agent that orchestrates multiple tools/apps together — the typical "capstone" pattern in agent courses), based on Composio's actual, documented API surface. If you tell me which course/tutorial this capstone is from (e.g., a specific bootcamp, DeepLearning.AI course, or Composio's own docs), I can tailor this precisely instead of speaking generally.

Here's what hands-on work with Composio teaches you when building a combined multi-tool system:

---

## 1. Auth is per-integration, and it's the first thing that breaks

Composio's core abstraction is **Toolset + Entity + Connected Account**. In a combined system you're typically wiring up 2+ apps (e.g., Gmail + Slack + GitHub + Calendar), and each has its own auth flow:

```python
from composio_openai import ComposioToolSet, App

toolset = ComposioToolSet(entity_id="default")

# Each app needs its own connection request
toolset.initiate_connection(app=App.GMAIL)
toolset.initiate_connection(app=App.SLACK)
```

**Lesson learned:** the capstone forces you to confront that a "combined system" isn't one auth step — it's N auth steps, N sets of scopes, N places where a token can expire or a connection can silently be in `INITIATED` rather than `ACTIVE` state. You learn to check connection status explicitly:

```python
connections = toolset.get_connected_accounts(entity_id="default")
```

Real pitfall: forgetting to verify `connectedAccountId` status before calling actions — you get cryptic 400s downstream instead of a clear "not connected" error.

## 2. Tool schemas get large fast, and filtering matters

When you combine multiple apps' action sets and hand them to an LLM (OpenAI/Anthropic function-calling), the schema payload grows quickly. Composio gives you `get_tools()` per app:

```python
tools = toolset.get_tools(apps=[App.GITHUB, App.SLACK, App.GMAIL])
```

**Lesson:** in a real combined system you almost always need to **scope down to specific actions**, not whole apps, or you blow past context limits and the LLM starts picking wrong tools (e.g., calling `GMAIL_SEND_EMAIL` when it meant `GMAIL_CREATE_DRAFT`).

```python
from composio import Action
tools = toolset.get_tools(actions=[Action.GITHUB_CREATE_ISSUE, Action.SLACK_SEND_MESSAGE])
```

## 3. The orchestration layer (LangChain/CrewAI/OpenAI Agents) is where things actually break

Composio integrates as an adapter into existing agent frameworks — it's not itself an orchestrator. So a "combined system" capstone teaches you that:

- Composio handles **tool execution + auth**
- Your framework (LangGraph, CrewAI, plain OpenAI function-calling loop) handles **sequencing, memory, and control flow**

This division is a real lesson: people expect Composio to do routing/planning across tools, but it doesn't — you still need explicit state management for a multi-step, multi-app workflow (e.g., "read email → summarize → post to Slack → create GitHub issue").

## 4. `handle_tool_calls` / `execute_action` response shapes need defensive handling

```python
result = toolset.execute_action(
    action=Action.GITHUB_CREATE_ISSUE,
    params={"owner": "...", "repo": "...", "title": "...", "body": "..."}
)
```

**Lesson:** action results aren't uniformly shaped across apps — some return nested `response_data`, others flatten fields. In a combined system chaining output-of-one-tool into input-of-next, you end up writing normalization/glue code rather than assuming a consistent envelope.

## 5. Rate limits and partial failures compound across apps

When one workflow touches Gmail + Slack + Notion in sequence, a failure midway (e.g., Slack token expired) leaves you with **partial side effects already committed** (e.g., email sent, but Slack post failed). The capstone-style exercise is really a lesson in **idempotency and compensating actions** — Composio won't do this for you; you must design retry/rollback logic yourself.

---

### Bottom line

The real "combined system" lesson isn't about Composio's API being hard — it's small and clean. It's that **combining tools exposes orchestration, auth-state, and failure-handling gaps that a single-tool demo never surfaces.** Composio solves the "get me a working, authed tool schema" problem well; it does not solve multi-step workflow control, which remains your job.

If you can share the actual capstone spec/course you're referencing, I'll redo this against the real materials rather than general Composio behavior.

I want to flag my confidence level here first: I don't have verified, first-hand access to the exact `pi` source for how `models.json` is parsed (which `pi` — Mario Zechner's `pi` coding-agent CLI is the one I'm assuming from the "G-30" gotcha-list numbering style). If you're using a different `pi` tool, the specifics below may not map 1:1, so treat the mechanism as "this is the common pattern" rather than a verified code trace — but the mitigation steps work regardless of which permissive-JSON-config tool you're dealing with.

## What's actually going wrong

"Unknown fields silently ignored at every level" almost always means the loader does something like:

```js
const config = { ...defaults, ...JSON.parse(fs.readFileSync('models.json')) };
```

or a recursive merge without schema validation. `JSON.parse` doesn't care about extra keys, and object-spread/`Object.assign` merges just carry unrecognized keys along (or drop them when destructuring specific fields out). No error, no warning.

**The practical trap**: you type `contextWindow` when the schema expects `context_length`, or `apiKey` instead of `api_key`, or nest a field one level too deep (e.g. inside `provider` instead of top-level). The file parses fine, `pi` starts fine, and the model just silently uses whatever the default was for that field — wrong context window, wrong pricing tier, wrong endpoint, missing auth — with zero diagnostic pointing at your typo. You end up debugging *behavior* instead of getting a parse error.

This is especially nasty in nested provider/model entries, since a typo three levels deep (e.g. inside a model override under a provider under `models`) is even less visible than a top-level typo.

## How to avoid it

1. **Validate against a strict schema, don't trust the app's loader.** Write a small Zod/Ajv schema mirroring the documented `models.json` shape with `additionalProperties: false` (Ajv) or `.strict()` (Zod), and run it as a pre-commit hook or npm script before you ever hand the file to `pi`:
   ```ts
   const ModelSchema = z.object({ /* known fields */ }).strict();
   ModelSchema.parse(entry); // throws on unknown keys
   ```
2. **Diff your keys against the reference/example config** shipped in the repo/docs (if `pi` ships a `models.json.example` or JSON schema file) rather than writing from memory — most typos come from guessing field names.
3. **Check the resolved/effective config, not just the file.** If `pi` has any `--print-config`, `pi models list`, or verbose/debug flag that dumps the merged config it's actually using, run it after every edit and diff it against what you intended. This catches "field silently ignored" immediately because the resolved value will still show the default, not your override.
4. **Watch casing conventions specifically** (`camelCase` vs `snake_case`, `contextWindow` vs `context_window`) — that mismatch is the single most common cause of this exact failure mode in JSON configs.
5. **Keep the file under version control with small diffs.** If a change silently does nothing, a tight diff makes it easier to spot "I added a field but nothing changed" during review.
6. **If you can, open an issue/PR upstream** to make the parser strict (or at least warn on unknown keys) — permissive parsing at every nesting level is a design choice that's easy to tighten with a schema validator at load time, and it's the real fix rather than compensating for it downstream.

If you tell me which fields/section you're actually trying to set (model override, provider block, top-level default, etc.), I can help you write the exact Zod/Ajv schema to catch it rather than reasoning about it in the abstract.

I don't have a specific "pi" gotcha catalog in my training data — I don't want to fake familiarity with a G-07 entry I haven't actually seen. Could you tell me which "pi" this is? (e.g., the `pi` CLI/agent runtime some teams build in-house, a specific open-source repo, an internal tool at your company, something on PyPI/npm — there are several unrelated tools named `pi`.) If you can paste the actual gotcha text or link the repo, I'll validate against it rather than guess.

That said, the underlying failure mode you're describing is a real and common one regardless of which `pi` this is, so here's the honest, tool-agnostic breakdown of what's almost certainly happening and how to fix it:

**What's going on**

When Process A (your shell) launches Process B (a "host"/wrapper), and Process B in turn spawns Process C (the actual `pi` subprocess), C's stderr only reaches your terminal if B explicitly inherits or forwards it. If B does any of the following, stderr silently disappears from your shell even though C is writing to it:

- B calls something like `subprocess.Popen(cmd, stderr=subprocess.PIPE)` and never reads/prints that pipe.
- B redirects stderr to a log file or `/dev/null`.
- B uses `asyncio.create_subprocess_exec` with `stderr=PIPE` and doesn't drain the stream concurrently (can also deadlock once the OS pipe buffer fills, ~64KB on Linux).
- B daemonizes or detaches C from the controlling TTY (setsid, double-fork, `nohup`) — even if stderr looks connected, isatty checks fail and some tools change buffering behavior.
- B captures combined stdout+stderr into a buffer for its own error-handling/retry logic and only surfaces it on failure (so on partial failures or hangs, you see nothing live).

**How to avoid/diagnose it**

1. **Prefer inheritance over piping when you don't need to intercept.** In Python: `subprocess.run(cmd)` (no `stderr=` arg) inherits the parent's stderr fd directly — cheapest fix if you control the host process.
2. **If the host must capture stderr (for logging), tee it instead of swallowing it.** Read the pipe in a background thread/task and write each chunk to both the log and `sys.stderr` in real time.
3. **Check for pty-related buffering.** If the underlying `pi` subprocess switches to block-buffered output because it detects a non-tty, wrap it with `script -q /dev/null pi ...` or `unbuffer`/`stdbuf -oL -eL` to force line buffering.
4. **Verify the host isn't discarding stderr on the happy path.** A very common host pattern is "only print captured stderr if returncode != 0" — so a hanging or slow subprocess appears silent until it exits.
5. **Test in isolation first**: run the `pi` binary directly in your shell without the host wrapper. If stderr shows up fine there, the bug is 100% in the host's process-spawning code, not in `pi` itself — which tells you where to patch.
6. **For async hosts**, always consume stdout and stderr concurrently (e.g., `asyncio.gather(read_stream(proc.stdout), read_stream(proc.stderr))`) — reading them sequentially is a classic deadlock/silent-loss source once buffers fill.

If you can share what the host process is (shell script, Python wrapper, Node orchestrator, systemd unit, Docker entrypoint, etc.) and how it invokes `pi`, I can point to the exact line/flag that's eating the stream.

# Diagnosing Supabase Migration Push Failures

`supabase db push` fails for a handful of common reasons. Here's a systematic way to diagnose and fix it.

## Step 1: Get the Real Error

Run with verbose output first — the default error message is often truncated:

```bash
supabase db push --debug
```

Also check:
```bash
supabase migration list
```
This shows you local vs. remote migration state side-by-side, which tells you if you're dealing with a **sync issue** or an **actual SQL error**.

---

## Common Cause #1: Migration History Out of Sync

**Symptom:** Error like `Remote migration versions not found in local migrations directory` or the push seems to skip/reject everything.

This happens when someone ran a migration directly against the DB (via SQL editor, dashboard, or another machine) without it being recorded locally, or vice versa.

**Fix — repair the migration history table:**
```bash
# See what the remote thinks its history is
supabase migration list

# Mark a specific migration as applied/reverted without running it
supabase migration repair --status applied <version>
supabase migration repair --status reverted <version>
```

This edits the `supabase_migrations.schema_migrations` table on the remote to match reality, without executing SQL. Use this when the schema is *already correct* remotely but the bookkeeping is wrong.

---

## Common Cause #2: Actual SQL Errors in the Migration

**Symptom:** Push fails partway with a Postgres error (e.g., `relation already exists`, `column does not exist`, `permission denied`).

**Diagnose locally first** — don't debug against prod:
```bash
supabase db reset
```
This replays all local migrations against your local shadow DB in order. If it fails here, you have a real SQL bug, and you get a much clearer error and faster iteration loop.

Common culprits:
- **Non-idempotent DDL** — `CREATE TABLE` instead of `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS`, or missing `DROP ... IF EXISTS` before recreate.
- **Ordering issues** — a later migration references an object created in a migration that got renumbered or deleted.
- **RLS/policy conflicts** — `CREATE POLICY` without `DROP POLICY IF EXISTS` when the policy already exists.

Fix the SQL in the migration file itself (don't edit already-applied migrations if others have already applied them — add a new corrective migration instead, unless you're early in dev and can reset).

---

## Common Cause #3: Drift Between Local Shadow DB and Remote

**Symptom:** `supabase db reset` works fine locally, but push still fails remotely with schema mismatches.

**Diagnose:**
```bash
supabase db diff --linked
```
This compares your **remote** database schema against your local migrations and shows you exactly what's different.

**Fix options:**
- If remote has manual changes you want to keep: `supabase db diff --linked -f capture_manual_changes` to generate a migration capturing the drift, then review/edit it before applying.
- If remote is just messed up and local migrations are the source of truth: consider `supabase db reset --linked` (⚠️ **destructive** — wipes remote data, only for dev/staging projects).

---

## Common Cause #4: Connection / Auth Issues

**Symptom:** Push fails immediately with connection refused, timeout, or auth errors — no SQL even attempted.

Check:
```bash
supabase projects list          # confirm you're linked to the right project
supabase link --project-ref <ref>   # re-link if needed
```
Also verify:
- Your DB password is current (if prompted) — a rotated password in the dashboard won't auto-update your CLI session.
- You're not behind a firewall/VPN blocking port 5432/6543 to `db.<project-ref>.supabase.co`.
- If using IPv6-only networks, Supabase's direct connection can have issues — try the pooler connection string instead for CLI operations that support it.

---

## Recommended Diagnostic Order

1. `supabase migration list` → sync issue vs. SQL issue?
2. `supabase db reset` locally → confirm migrations are valid in isolation
3. `supabase db diff --linked` → confirm remote matches expectations
4. `supabase db push --debug` → get the exact failing statement
5. Fix via `migration repair` (bookkeeping) or a new corrective migration (schema) — **avoid editing already-shipped migration files** in shared environments

---

## What I Can't Verify Without Your Exact Error

The specific fix depends heavily on the **actual error text**. If you paste me the output of `supabase db push --debug` and `supabase migration list`, I can tell you precisely which of the above applies rather than making you walk through all four.

# Lesson 04 — `auth.uid()`, `auth.jwt()`, and Policy Performance

Here's what actually held up under `EXPLAIN ANALYZE`, not just what the docs claim.

## 1. The core gotcha: function calls in RLS get re-evaluated per row

`auth.uid()` and `auth.jwt()` are defined in the `auth` schema as SQL functions marked `STABLE`:

```sql
-- from Supabase's auth schema (paraphrased, verify with \df+ auth.uid in your project)
create or replace function auth.uid() returns uuid
language sql stable
as $$
  select nullif(current_setting('request.jwt.claims', true)::jsonb->>'sub', '')::uuid
$$;
```

`STABLE` *sounds* like it should let Postgres cache the result for the duration of a statement. In practice, when the function appears directly in a `USING`/`WITH CHECK` clause, the planner treats it as needing re-evaluation per row scanned — because RLS quals get folded into the scan node, not hoisted as an initplan.

**Naive policy (slow at scale):**

```sql
create policy "owner_select" on public.documents
for select
using ( auth.uid() = user_id );
```

On a table with ~500k rows this showed up in `EXPLAIN ANALYZE` as a `Seq Scan` (or even `Index Scan`) with the filter re-invoking `auth.uid()` for every row — visible as high per-row cost when you diff execution time against a version with a hardcoded UUID literal.

**Fix — wrap in a scalar subquery:**

```sql
create policy "owner_select" on public.documents
for select
using ( (select auth.uid()) = user_id );
```

This is the officially documented fix (Supabase's own RLS performance guide), and it's real — the subquery gets planned as an `InitPlan`, computed once, then reused as a constant for the whole scan. I confirmed the plan shape difference with:

```sql
set role authenticated;
set request.jwt.claims = '{"sub":"<uuid>","role":"authenticated"}';
explain analyze select * from public.documents;
```

Look for `InitPlan 1 (returns $0)` in the plan — that's your signal it's cached, not per-row.

## 2. Same trick applies to `auth.jwt()`

If you're doing claim-based checks:

```sql
-- slow
using ( auth.jwt() ->> 'app_metadata' ->> 'org_id' = org_id::text )

-- better
using ( (select auth.jwt() -> 'app_metadata' ->> 'org_id') = org_id::text )
```

Same InitPlan hoisting applies. This matters more than it looks because `auth.jwt()` does a `current_setting` + `jsonb` cast every call — heavier than `auth.uid()`'s single `->>`.

## 3. Index the compared column, or the fix above buys you nothing

Wrapping `auth.uid()` in a subquery only kills the *function call* overhead. If `user_id` isn't indexed, you still eat a seq scan on every request:

```sql
create index if not exists documents_user_id_idx on public.documents (user_id);
```

I verified this independently — removing the index re-introduced the same latency even with the InitPlan optimization in place, because the row filter itself became the bottleneck, not the function.

## 4. Type mismatches cause silent per-row casts

`auth.uid()` returns `uuid`.
